message:
fix(util): modulo bias, Hasher contract, seed correlation in random module

- DetRng::next_usize: replace modulo with rejection sampling to eliminate
  statistical bias (matching the correct Cx::random_usize implementation)
- DetHasher: remove write_u8/write_u64 specializations that violated the
  Hasher trait contract (produced different hashes than write() for same bytes)
- ThreadLocalEntropy::for_thread: use additive constant + mix_seed() to
  prevent correlated seeds when global_seed is 0
- DetHashMap/DetHashSet: correct misleading doc claiming deterministic
  iteration order (HashMap does not guarantee this)

Closes bd-cp6u.

Co-Authored-By: Claude Opus 4.5 <noreply@anthropic.com>

Created comprehensive benchmark suite: benches/raptorq_benchmark.rs

### GF(256) Primitives Baseline
| Operation | 64B | 256B | 1KB | 4KB | 16KB |
|-----------|-----|------|-----|-----|------|
| add_slice (XOR) | 10.9 GiB/s | 19.3 GiB/s | 22.8 GiB/s | 23.4 GiB/s | 21.8 GiB/s |
| mul_slice | 1.1 GiB/s | 1.1 GiB/s | 0.9 GiB/s | 1.2 GiB/s | 1.1 GiB/s |
| addmul_slice | 1.0 GiB/s | 1.0 GiB/s | 1.1 GiB/s | 1.0 GiB/s | 1.1 GiB/s |

### Key Findings
1. XOR (add_slice) is memory-bound at ~20-24 GiB/s - SIMD autovectorized by LLVM
2. addmul_slice is THE hot path at ~1 GiB/s (20x slower than XOR due to table lookups)
3. Branchless optimization tested but reverted - showed mixed results:
   - +7% at 4KB but -8% to -16% at smaller sizes
   - Branch predictor performs well when data is mostly non-zero (typical case)

### End-to-End Throughput
- Encode (k=32, 1KB symbols): ~36 MiB/s
- Decode (source only): ~70 MiB/s
- Decode (repair only): ~13 MiB/s (Gaussian elimination is O(n^3))

### Next Steps
- bd-35lp: Safe SIMD XOR acceleration (potential 2-4x for addmul with gather)
- bd-1l7k: Buffer reuse to reduce allocations

 {"id":"bd-1kg0","title":"WebSocket handshake: HTTP upgrade negotiation","description":"# WebSocket Handshake Implementation

## Goal

Implement the WebSocket opening handshake (HTTP upgrade) for both client and server roles.

## Background

WebSocket connections start as HTTP/1.1 and upgrade via the Upgrade header mechanism.

### Client Request
```http
GET /chat HTTP/1.1
Host: server.example.com
Upgrade: websocket
Connection: Upgrade
Sec-WebSocket-Key: dGhlIHNhbXBsZSBub25jZQ==
Sec-WebSocket-Version: 13
Sec-WebSocket-Protocol: chat, superchat
Sec-WebSocket-Extensions: permessage-deflate
```

### Server Response
```http
HTTP/1.1 101 Switching Protocols
Upgrade: websocket
Connection: Upgrade
Sec-WebSocket-Accept: s3pPLMBiTxaQ9kYGzzhZRbK+xOo=
Sec-WebSocket-Protocol: chat
```

## Types to Implement

### ClientHandshake
```rust
pub struct ClientHandshake {
    /// WebSocket URL (ws:// or wss://)
    url: Url,
    
    /// Random 16-byte key, base64 encoded
    key: String,
    
    /// Requested subprotocols (optional)
    protocols: Vec<String>,
    
    /// Requested extensions (optional)
    extensions: Vec<String>,
    
    /// Additional headers
    headers: HeaderMap,
}

impl ClientHandshake {
    /// Generate the HTTP upgrade request
    pub fn request(&self) -> Request<()>;
    
    /// Validate server response
    pub fn validate_response(&self, response: &Response<()>) -> Result<(), HandshakeError>;
}
```

### ServerHandshake
```rust
pub struct ServerHandshake {
    /// Supported subprotocols
    supported_protocols: Vec<String>,
    
    /// Supported extensions
    supported_extensions: Vec<String>,
    
    /// Custom validation callback
    validator: Option<Box<dyn Fn(&Request<()>) -> bool>>,
}

impl ServerHandshake {
    /// Validate client request and generate response
    pub fn accept(&self, request: &Request<()>) -> Result<Response<()>, HandshakeError>;
    
    /// Reject with custom status code
    pub fn reject(status: StatusCode) -> Response<()>;
}
```

### HandshakeError
```rust
pub enum HandshakeError {
    /// Invalid HTTP request
    InvalidRequest(String),
    
    /// Missing required header
    MissingHeader(&'static str),
    
    /// Invalid Sec-WebSocket-Key
    InvalidKey,
    
    /// Invalid Sec-WebSocket-Accept (response validation)
    InvalidAccept,
    
    /// Unsupported WebSocket version
    UnsupportedVersion(u8),
    
    /// Protocol negotiation failed
    ProtocolMismatch,
    
    /// Server rejected upgrade
    Rejected(StatusCode),
}
```

## Sec-WebSocket-Accept Calculation

The accept key is computed as:
```rust
fn compute_accept_key(client_key: &str) -> String {
    use sha1::{Sha1, Digest};
    use base64::Engine;
    
    const GUID: &str = "258EAFA5-E914-47DA-95CA-C5AB0DC85B11";
    
    let mut hasher = Sha1::new();
    hasher.update(client_key.as_bytes());
    hasher.update(GUID.as_bytes());
    let hash = hasher.finalize();
    
    base64::engine::general_purpose::STANDARD.encode(hash)
}
```

## URL Parsing

```rust
pub fn parse_ws_url(url: &str) -> Result<(String, u16, String, bool), UrlError> {
    // ws://host:port/path -> (host, port, path, false)
    // wss://host:port/path -> (host, port, path, true)
    // Default ports: ws=80, wss=443
}
```

## Client Flow

```rust
pub async fn client_handshake(
    cx: &Cx,
    stream: TcpStream,
    handshake: ClientHandshake,
) -> Outcome<WebSocket, HandshakeError> {
    // 1. Send HTTP upgrade request
    let request = handshake.request();
    stream.write_all(cx, &request.to_bytes()).await?;
    
    // 2. Read HTTP response
    let response = read_http_response(cx, &mut stream).await?;
    
    // 3. Validate response
    handshake.validate_response(&response)?;
    
    // 4. Return WebSocket on success
    Ok(WebSocket::from_upgraded(stream, Role::Client))
}
```

## Server Flow

```rust
pub async fn server_handshake(
    cx: &Cx,
    stream: TcpStream,
    config: ServerHandshake,
) -> Outcome<(WebSocket, Request<()>), HandshakeError> {
    // 1. Read HTTP request
    let request = read_http_request(cx, &mut stream).await?;
    
    // 2. Validate and generate response
    let response = config.accept(&request)?;
    
    // 3. Send response
    stream.write_all(cx, &response.to_bytes()).await?;
    
    // 4. Return WebSocket and original request
    Ok((WebSocket::from_upgraded(stream, Role::Server), request))
}
```

## TLS Integration

For wss:// URLs:
```rust
pub async fn connect_wss(
    cx: &Cx,
    url: &str,
    tls_config: TlsConfig,
) -> Outcome<WebSocket, WsError> {
    let (host, port, path, _) = parse_ws_url(url)?;
    
    // 1. TCP connect
    let tcp = TcpStream::connect(cx, (host.as_str(), port)).await?;
    
    // 2. TLS handshake
    let tls = TlsConnector::new(tls_config)
        .connect(cx, &host, tcp)
        .await?;
    
    // 3. WebSocket handshake
    client_handshake(cx, tls, handshake).await
}
```

## Testing

### Unit Tests
- Accept key computation
- URL parsing
- Request/response generation
- Validation logic

### Integration Tests
- Successful handshake
- Rejected handshake
- Invalid key
- Subprotocol negotiation
- Extension negotiation (basic)

### Interop Tests
- Connect to echo.websocket.org
- Connect to local test server

## Acceptance Criteria

- [ ] Client handshake works
- [ ] Server handshake works
- [ ] TLS (wss://) supported
- [ ] Subprotocol negotiation
- [ ] Extension negotiation (basic)
- [ ] Error handling complete
- [ ] Interop with standard servers

 {"id":"bd-1sx3","title":"Epic: Combinator Implementation (join!/race!/select!)","description":"# Epic: Combinator Implementation

## Overview

Implement the structured concurrency combinators that compose concurrent operations: join (wait for all), race (wait for first), select (wait for any with continuation), and related patterns.

## Background

Asupersync's combinators differ from typical async combinator libraries:
1. **Cancel-correct**: Losers in a race are properly cancelled and drained
2. **Two-phase aware**: Combinator results use Outcome, not Result
3. **Region-integrated**: Combinators operate within structured concurrency regions
4. **Obligation-safe**: Obligations are properly resolved across all branches

## Combinators to Implement

### join!
Wait for all futures, return tuple of results:
```rust
let (a, b, c) = join!(future_a, future_b, future_c).await;
```

Semantics:
- All futures run concurrently
- If any fails, others continue (collect all Outcomes)
- If any panics, others continue
- Final Outcome is the 'worst' (highest severity)

### race!
Wait for first to complete, cancel losers:
```rust
let winner: Either3<A, B, C> = race!(future_a, future_b, future_c).await;
```

Semantics:
- All futures run concurrently
- First to complete is the winner
- Losers are cancelled (request → drain → finalize)
- Wait for losers to fully drain before returning

### select!
Pattern-match on first completion with cancellation:
```rust
select! {
    a = future_a => handle_a(a),
    b = future_b => handle_b(b),
    else => handle_none(),
}
```

### timeout!
Race a future against a deadline:
```rust
let result = timeout!(Duration::from_secs(5), operation).await;
```

### try_join!
Like join but short-circuit on first error:
```rust
let (a, b, c) = try_join!(future_a, future_b, future_c).await?;
```

## Implementation Approach

1. **Macro-based**: Procedural macros for ergonomic syntax
2. **Type-safe**: Full type inference for all branches
3. **Cancelable**: All branches respond to cancellation
4. **Budget-aware**: Track poll counts across branches

## Dependencies

- Cancellation system must be complete
- Region tree must support concurrent children
- Obligation tracking for two-phase operations

## Success Criteria

- All combinators work correctly
- Losers properly drained in races
- Obligations resolved across all branches
- Type inference works without annotations
- Lab runtime tests deterministic
- Documentation with examples

## References

 {"id":"bd-2auz","title":"Filesystem E2E test suite: io_uring and directory operation tests","description":"# Filesystem E2E Test Suite

 {"id":"bd-2c2i","title":"Lease manager: time-bounded resource claims","description":"# Lease Manager Implementation

## Goal

Implement a lease system for time-bounded claims on remote resources, preventing orphaned resources when nodes fail.

## Background

In distributed systems, resources can be orphaned when:
1. Client crashes without releasing resource
2. Network partition prevents release message
3. Client hangs indefinitely

Leases solve this: resources are only held for a bounded time. If not renewed, they're automatically released.

## Core Types

### Lease
```rust
pub struct Lease {
    /// Unique lease identifier
    id: LeaseId,
    
    /// Resource being leased
    resource: ResourceId,
    
    /// Holder of the lease
    holder: NodeId,
    
    /// When lease was granted
    granted_at: LogicalTime,
    
    /// When lease expires (must renew before)
    expires_at: LogicalTime,
    
    /// Lease state
    state: LeaseState,
}

pub enum LeaseState {
    Active,
    Expiring,  // Close to expiry, renewal needed
    Expired,   // Resource released
    Released,  // Holder explicitly released
}
```

### LeaseId
```rust
pub struct LeaseId {
    /// Resource ID
    resource: ResourceId,
    
    /// Unique grant nonce
    nonce: u64,
    
    /// Granting node
    grantor: NodeId,
}
```

## LeaseManager

```rust
pub struct LeaseManager {
    /// Active leases held by this node
    held: HashMap<LeaseId, Lease>,
    
    /// Leases granted by this node
    granted: HashMap<LeaseId, Lease>,
    
    /// Background renewal task
    renewal_task: TaskHandle,
    
    /// Clock source
    clock: Box<dyn LogicalClock>,
}

impl LeaseManager {
    /// Request a lease on a resource
    async fn acquire(&mut self, resource: ResourceId, duration: Duration) -> Result<Lease, LeaseError>;
    
    /// Renew an existing lease
    async fn renew(&mut self, lease: &mut Lease, duration: Duration) -> Result<(), LeaseError>;
    
    /// Release a lease early
    async fn release(&mut self, lease: Lease) -> Result<(), LeaseError>;
    
    /// Check if lease is still valid
    fn is_valid(&self, lease: &Lease) -> bool;
    
    /// Get time until expiry
    fn time_until_expiry(&self, lease: &Lease) -> Duration;
}
```

## Renewal Strategy

```rust
pub struct RenewalConfig {
    /// Renew when this fraction of lease time remains
    renewal_threshold: f64,  // e.g., 0.5 = renew at half-time
    
    /// Retry interval if renewal fails
    retry_interval: Duration,
    
    /// Max retries before giving up
    max_retries: u32,
}
```

Background task renews leases automatically:
1. Monitor all held leases
2. When time_until_expiry < threshold, renew
3. On failure, retry with backoff
4. If max_retries exceeded, trigger lease_lost callback

## Lease Lost Handling

When a lease expires without renewal:
```rust
pub trait LeaseObserver {
    /// Called when lease cannot be renewed
    fn on_lease_lost(&self, lease: LeaseId, reason: LeaseError);
}
```

Default behavior: cancel tasks/actors using the leased resource.

## Clock Considerations

Leases use logical time, not wall time:
- Prevents issues from clock skew
- Lease duration is 'messages' not 'seconds'
- Renewal piggybacks on normal communication

Alternative: Hybrid logical clocks for bounded skew tolerance.

## Wire Protocol

### LeaseRequest
```rust
struct LeaseRequest {
    resource: ResourceId,
    duration: Duration,
    holder: NodeId,
}
```

### LeaseResponse
```rust
enum LeaseResponse {
    Granted { lease: Lease },
    Denied { reason: LeaseDenied },
}

enum LeaseDenied {
    ResourceNotFound,
    AlreadyLeased { holder: NodeId, expires: LogicalTime },
    ResourceLimits,
}
```

## Integration Points

- RemoteHandle holds Lease for remote task
- ActorRef for remote actors has Lease
- Distributed regions use leases for membership
- Saga steps may acquire leases on resources

## Testing

- Lease acquisition and release
- Automatic renewal in background
- Lease expiry when holder crashes
- Multiple lease requests (conflict handling)
- Lab runtime with simulated network delays

## Acceptance Criteria

- [ ] Lease and LeaseId types
- [ ] LeaseManager with acquire/renew/release
- [ ] Background renewal task
- [ ] LeaseState transitions
- [ ] LeaseObserver callback
- [ ] Wire protocol messages
- [ ] Logical clock integration
- [ ] Tests for all scenarios","status":"closed","priority":2,"issue_type":"task","created_at":"2026-01-31T21:22:06.196253260Z","created_by":"ubuntu","updated_at":"2026-02-02T06:42:55.317154352Z","closed_at":"2026-02-02T06:42:55.317069584Z","close_reason":"done","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0,"labels":["distributed","leases","phase4"],"dependencies":[{"issue_id":"bd-2c2i","depends_on_id":"bd-1jis","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-02-03T00:02:09Z","created_by":"import"},{"issue_id":"bd-2c2i","depends_on_id":"bd-3sst","type":"parent-child","created_at":"2026-02-03T00:02:09Z","created_by":"import"}]}

 {"id":"bd-2dbf","title":"HPACK: Huffman invalid padding silently accepted (FIXED)","description":"# Bug Fix: Huffman Invalid Padding (COMPLETED)

## Status: FIXED in session 2026-01-31

## Location
`src/http/h2/hpack.rs` - huffman_decode function

## Vulnerability
The Huffman decoder silently accepted invalid padding (not all 1s) at the end
of encoded data. Per RFC 7541, padding must be the most-significant bits of EOS.
Invalid padding could indicate encoding errors or malicious data.

## Original Code
```rust
// End of decoding - didn't check padding validity
Ok(String::from_utf8(output).map_err(|_| H2Error::compression("invalid UTF-8"))?)
```

## Fix Applied
Validate padding bits are all 1s:

```rust
// After decoding, check remaining bits are valid padding
if bit_offset > 0 {
    // Remaining bits should be all 1s (padding is MSBs of EOS symbol)
    let padding_mask = (1u8 << (8 - bit_offset)) - 1;
    let padding_bits = current_byte & padding_mask;
    if padding_bits != padding_mask {
        return Err(H2Error::compression("invalid Huffman padding"));
    }
}
```

## Testing Required
- test_huffman_decode_invalid_padding
- test_huffman_decode_valid_padding
- Fuzz test: Random byte sequences","status":"closed","priority":0,"issue_type":"bug","created_at":"2026-02-01T02:57:13.121666105Z","created_by":"ubuntu","updated_at":"2026-02-01T02:57:31.914317531Z","closed_at":"2026-02-01T02:57:31.914150501Z","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0,"dependencies":[{"issue_id":"bd-2dbf","depends_on_id":"bd-1bic","type":"parent-child","created_at":"2026-02-03T00:02:09Z","created_by":"import"}]}

 {"id":"bd-cbz","title":"Implement two-phase mutex with guard obligations","description":"## Purpose
Implement a cancel-safe async mutex where lock guards are obligations. This ensures locks are always released even under cancellation.

## Why Async Mutex?
Async code cannot hold `std::sync::Mutex` guards across await points (not Send in general). Async mutex allows:
- Holding lock across await
- Cancel-safe lock acquisition
- Obligation tracking for lock release

## Two-Phase Mutex Model

```rust
pub struct Mutex<T> {
    locked: AtomicBool,
    waiters: WaitQueue,
    data: UnsafeCell<T>,
}

pub struct MutexGuard<'a, T> {
    mutex: &'a Mutex<T>,
    obligation_id: ObligationId,
}

impl<T> Mutex<T> {
    /// Create new mutex.
    pub fn new(value: T) -> Self;
    
    /// Lock the mutex. Cancel-safe during wait.
    pub async fn lock(&self, cx: &mut Cx<'_>) -> MutexGuard<'_, T>;
    
    /// Try to lock without waiting.
    pub fn try_lock(&self) -> Option<MutexGuard<'_, T>>;
    
    /// Get mutable reference (if exclusively owned).
    pub fn get_mut(&mut self) -> &mut T;
}

impl<T> Deref for MutexGuard<'_, T> {
    type Target = T;
    fn deref(&self) -> &T { /* ... */ }
}

impl<T> DerefMut for MutexGuard<'_, T> {
    fn deref_mut(&mut self) -> &mut T { /* ... */ }
}

impl<T> Drop for MutexGuard<'_, T> {
    fn drop(&mut self) {
        // Unlock mutex
        // Resolve obligation as Committed
        // Wake next waiter
    }
}
```

## Guard as Obligation
Like semaphore permits, mutex guards are obligations:
- **Created**: When lock acquired
- **Committed**: When guard dropped (unlocked)

## Two-Phase Semantics
- **Phase 1**: Wait for lock availability (cancel-safe)
- **Phase 2**: Acquire lock (creates obligation)

Cancellation during wait is clean - no lock held.

## Fairness and Priority
Options for lock scheduling:
1. **FIFO**: Waiters serviced in order (prevents starvation)
2. **Priority**: Higher priority tasks get lock first
3. **Barging**: New arrivals can barge if lucky (not recommended)

For Asupersync, FIFO is default. Priority can be added via separate API.

## Deadlock Prevention
The spec does not prevent deadlocks at runtime, but:
- Lab runtime can detect deadlock (cycle in wait graph)
- Timeout wrappers can bound wait time
- Design patterns (lock ordering) prevent in application

## Common Pattern: Shared State
```rust
let state = Mutex::new(SharedState::default());

scope.spawn(cx, |cx| async move {
    let mut guard = state.lock(cx).await;
    guard.counter += 1;
    // guard dropped, lock released
});
```

## Cancellation Handling
| Scenario | Behavior |
|----------|----------|
| Cancel during lock wait | Clean abort, lock not held |
| Cancel while holding lock | Guard dropped, lock released |
| Panic while holding lock | Guard dropped (unwind safety) |

## Invariant Support
- **Obligation tracking**: Guards are obligations
- **No deadlock leaks**: Guards always release on drop
- **Cancel-safety**: Wait is interruptible

## Testing Requirements
1. Basic lock/unlock
2. Contention (multiple waiters)
3. Cancel during wait
4. try_lock success and failure
5. FIFO ordering verification
6. Deadlock detection (lab runtime)
7. Guard deref operations

## References
- asupersync_plan_v4.md: §6.5 Two-Phase Operations
- tokio::sync::Mutex
- async-std::sync::Mutex
- parking_lot mutex

## Acceptance Criteria
- Mutex acquisition uses a two-phase / obligation-based protocol so cancellation cannot silently lose ownership.
- Dropping a guard/permit has deterministic semantics (release/abort) and is trace-visible.
- Unit/E2E tests cover cancellation while waiting, while holding the guard, and region close interactions.

 {"id":"bd-cdmp","title":"Implement trace file format and serialization","description":"## Overview

Implement the binary file format for persisting recorded traces and loading them for replay.

## Background

Traces need to be saved to disk for later replay. The format should be:
- Compact (traces can be large for long tests)
- Versioned (forward compatible as schema evolves)
- Streamable (can start replay before full load)

## File Format Design

### Header
```
Magic: "ASUPERTRACE" (11 bytes)
Version: u16 (format version)
Flags: u16 (compression, etc.)
Metadata length: u32
Metadata: TraceMetadata (bincode)
```

### Events Section
```
Event count: u64
Events: [TraceEvent] (bincode, optionally compressed)
```

### Optional Compression
- Support LZ4 compression for large traces
- Compression flag in header
- Chunk-based for streaming decompression

## API

```rust
pub struct TraceFile {
    path: PathBuf,
}

impl TraceFile {
    pub fn create(path: impl AsRef<Path>) -> io::Result<TraceWriter>;
    pub fn open(path: impl AsRef<Path>) -> io::Result<TraceReader>;
}

pub struct TraceWriter { /* ... */ }
impl TraceWriter {
    pub fn write_metadata(&mut self, meta: &TraceMetadata) -> io::Result<()>;
    pub fn write_event(&mut self, event: &TraceEvent) -> io::Result<()>;
    pub fn finish(self) -> io::Result<()>;
}

pub struct TraceReader { /* ... */ }
impl TraceReader {
    pub fn metadata(&self) -> &TraceMetadata;
    pub fn events(&self) -> impl Iterator<Item = io::Result<TraceEvent>>;
}
```

## Acceptance Criteria

- [ ] File format is documented
- [ ] Version field allows future schema changes
- [ ] Round-trip test: write then read preserves all data
- [ ] Large trace test (10k+ events) works correctly
- [ ] Optional compression reduces file size by 50%+","status":"closed","priority":2,"issue_type":"task","assignee":"CalmMeadow","owner":"jeff141421@gmail.com","created_at":"2026-01-18T18:01:29.962338187Z","created_by":"Dicklesworthstone","updated_at":"2026-01-20T23:52:39.351262439Z","closed_at":"2026-01-20T23:52:39.349802790Z","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0,"dependencies":[{"issue_id":"bd-cdmp","depends_on_id":"bd-u8yo","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-02-03T00:01:55Z","created_by":"import"}]}

 {"id":"bd-cpqu","title":"[EPIC] Chaos Testing Mode for Lab Runtime","description":"# Overview

An extension to LabReactor that can inject random delays, spurious wakeups,
simulated I/O errors, and cancellations at configurable rates - to stress-test
application code's resilience.

## Why This Matters

Production systems face chaos constantly:
- Network partitions
- Slow responses  
- OOM kills
- Race conditions
- Random failures

A chaos mode lets developers find these bugs BEFORE production. Combined with
deterministic replay, failures become REPRODUCIBLE.

## How It Works

```rust
Lab::new()
    .with_chaos(ChaosConfig {
        cancel_probability: 0.01,      // 1% random cancellation
        delay_probability: 0.05,       // 5% random delay
        delay_range: 1ms..100ms,
        io_error_probability: 0.02,    // 2% I/O error
        wakeup_storm_probability: 0.001, // 0.1% spurious wakeups
        seed: 12345,                   // REPRODUCIBLE chaos!
    })
    .run(|cx| async {
        // Application code with injected chaos
    });

// On failure:
// "Test failed with seed 12345. Replay with same seed to reproduce."
```

## Chaos Types

1. **Random Cancellation**: Inject CancelRequested at random times
2. **Random Delays**: Add latency at poll points
3. **I/O Errors**: Fail I/O operations randomly
4. **Wakeup Storms**: Spurious wakeups to test idempotency
5. **Budget Exhaustion**: Randomly exhaust budgets
6. **Clock Skew**: Virtual time jumps (for distributed scenarios)

## Determinism is Key

The chaos MUST be deterministic:
- Same seed = same chaos sequence
- Same test = same failures
- Enables debugging of intermittent issues

This is the killer combination: chaos + deterministic replay.

## Integration with Existing Features

- Uses LabReactor's virtual time
- Uses LabReactor's deterministic scheduling
- Integrates with cancellation injection (#1)
- Reports via tracing (#2)
- Verified by oracles

## Success Criteria

- [ ] Configurable chaos parameters
- [ ] Deterministic with same seed
- [ ] All chaos types implemented
- [ ] Integrates with Lab infrastructure
- [ ] Clear failure reporting with seed

Implement the `Buf` and `BufMut` traits - the abstract interfaces for reading from and writing to buffers.

## Rationale

These traits enable:
- Generic codec implementations
- Zero-copy buffer chaining
- Efficient protocol parsing
- Interoperability with any buffer type

They are used by:
- tokio-util codecs (Framed, LengthDelimited, etc.)
- HTTP parsing in hyper
- gRPC message framing
- Database wire protocols

## Implementation

### Buf Trait (Reader)

```rust
// bytes/src/buf/mod.rs

/// Read bytes from a buffer.
///
/// This is the main abstraction for reading bytes. It provides
/// a cursor-like interface that advances through the buffer.
pub trait Buf {
    /// Returns the number of bytes remaining.
    fn remaining(&self) -> usize;

    /// Returns a slice of the next contiguous chunk.
    /// May return less than remaining() if buffer is fragmented.
    fn chunk(&self) -> &[u8];

    /// Advance the internal cursor by `cnt` bytes.
    ///
    /// # Panics
    /// Panics if `cnt > self.remaining()`.
    fn advance(&mut self, cnt: usize);

    // === Default implementations ===

    /// Returns true if there are bytes remaining.
    #[inline]
    fn has_remaining(&self) -> bool {
        self.remaining() > 0
    }

    /// Copy bytes to `dst`, advancing cursor.
    fn copy_to_slice(&mut self, dst: &mut [u8]) {
        assert!(self.remaining() >= dst.len(), "buffer underflow");

        let mut off = 0;
        while off < dst.len() {
            let chunk = self.chunk();
            let cnt = std::cmp::min(chunk.len(), dst.len() - off);
            dst[off..off + cnt].copy_from_slice(&chunk[..cnt]);
            self.advance(cnt);
            off += cnt;
        }
    }

    /// Get a u8, advancing cursor.
    fn get_u8(&mut self) -> u8 {
        assert!(self.remaining() >= 1, "buffer underflow");
        let val = self.chunk()[0];
        self.advance(1);
        val
    }

    /// Get an i8, advancing cursor.
    fn get_i8(&mut self) -> i8 {
        self.get_u8() as i8
    }

    /// Get a big-endian u16.
    fn get_u16(&mut self) -> u16 {
        let mut buf = [0u8; 2];
        self.copy_to_slice(&mut buf);
        u16::from_be_bytes(buf)
    }

    /// Get a little-endian u16.
    fn get_u16_le(&mut self) -> u16 {
        let mut buf = [0u8; 2];
        self.copy_to_slice(&mut buf);
        u16::from_le_bytes(buf)
    }

    /// Get a native-endian u16.
    fn get_u16_ne(&mut self) -> u16 {
        let mut buf = [0u8; 2];
        self.copy_to_slice(&mut buf);
        u16::from_ne_bytes(buf)
    }

    /// Get a big-endian i16.
    fn get_i16(&mut self) -> i16 {
        self.get_u16() as i16
    }

    /// Get a little-endian i16.
    fn get_i16_le(&mut self) -> i16 {
        self.get_u16_le() as i16
    }

    /// Get a big-endian u32.
    fn get_u32(&mut self) -> u32 {
        let mut buf = [0u8; 4];
        self.copy_to_slice(&mut buf);
        u32::from_be_bytes(buf)
    }

    /// Get a little-endian u32.
    fn get_u32_le(&mut self) -> u32 {
        let mut buf = [0u8; 4];
        self.copy_to_slice(&mut buf);
        u32::from_le_bytes(buf)
    }

    /// Get a big-endian i32.
    fn get_i32(&mut self) -> i32 {
        self.get_u32() as i32
    }

    /// Get a little-endian i32.
    fn get_i32_le(&mut self) -> i32 {
        self.get_u32_le() as i32
    }

    /// Get a big-endian u64.
    fn get_u64(&mut self) -> u64 {
        let mut buf = [0u8; 8];
        self.copy_to_slice(&mut buf);
        u64::from_be_bytes(buf)
    }

    /// Get a little-endian u64.
    fn get_u64_le(&mut self) -> u64 {
        let mut buf = [0u8; 8];
        self.copy_to_slice(&mut buf);
        u64::from_le_bytes(buf)
    }

    /// Get a big-endian i64.
    fn get_i64(&mut self) -> i64 {
        self.get_u64() as i64
    }

    /// Get a little-endian i64.
    fn get_i64_le(&mut self) -> i64 {
        self.get_u64_le() as i64
    }

    /// Get a big-endian u128.
    fn get_u128(&mut self) -> u128 {
        let mut buf = [0u8; 16];
        self.copy_to_slice(&mut buf);
        u128::from_be_bytes(buf)
    }

    /// Get a little-endian u128.
    fn get_u128_le(&mut self) -> u128 {
        let mut buf = [0u8; 16];
        self.copy_to_slice(&mut buf);
        u128::from_le_bytes(buf)
    }

    /// Get a big-endian f32.
    fn get_f32(&mut self) -> f32 {
        f32::from_bits(self.get_u32())
    }

    /// Get a little-endian f32.
    fn get_f32_le(&mut self) -> f32 {
        f32::from_bits(self.get_u32_le())
    }

    /// Get a big-endian f64.
    fn get_f64(&mut self) -> f64 {
        f64::from_bits(self.get_u64())
    }

    /// Get a little-endian f64.
    fn get_f64_le(&mut self) -> f64 {
        f64::from_bits(self.get_u64_le())
    }

    /// Chain this buffer with another.
    fn chain<U: Buf>(self, next: U) -> Chain<Self, U>
    where
        Self: Sized,
    {
        Chain::new(self, next)
    }

    /// Limit reading to first `limit` bytes.
    fn take(self, limit: usize) -> Take<Self>
    where
        Self: Sized,
    {
        Take::new(self, limit)
    }
}
```

### BufMut Trait (Writer)

```rust
// bytes/src/buf/buf_mut.rs

use std::mem::MaybeUninit;

/// Uninitialized slice wrapper for safe uninitialized writes.
#[repr(transparent)]
pub struct UninitSlice([MaybeUninit<u8>]);

impl UninitSlice {
    /// Create from a mutable slice of MaybeUninit bytes.
    pub fn from_raw_parts_mut(ptr: *mut MaybeUninit<u8>, len: usize) -> &'static mut Self {
        unsafe {
            &mut *(std::ptr::slice_from_raw_parts_mut(ptr, len) as *mut UninitSlice)
        }
    }

    /// Get raw pointer.
    pub fn as_mut_ptr(&mut self) -> *mut u8 {
        self.0.as_mut_ptr() as *mut u8
    }

    /// Get length.
    pub fn len(&self) -> usize {
        self.0.len()
    }

    /// Write a byte at index (unsafe - does not bounds check).
    pub unsafe fn write_byte(&mut self, index: usize, byte: u8) {
        self.0.get_unchecked_mut(index).write(byte);
    }
}

/// Write bytes to a buffer.
pub trait BufMut {
    /// Returns number of bytes that can be written.
    fn remaining_mut(&self) -> usize;

    /// Advance the write cursor by `cnt` bytes.
    ///
    /// # Safety
    /// The caller must have written `cnt` bytes to the buffer
    /// returned by `chunk_mut()`.
    unsafe fn advance_mut(&mut self, cnt: usize);

    /// Returns a mutable slice of uninitialized bytes.
    fn chunk_mut(&mut self) -> &mut UninitSlice;

    // === Default implementations ===

    /// Returns true if there is space remaining.
    #[inline]
    fn has_remaining_mut(&self) -> bool {
        self.remaining_mut() > 0
    }

    /// Put a slice into the buffer.
    fn put_slice(&mut self, src: &[u8]) {
        assert!(self.remaining_mut() >= src.len(), "buffer overflow");

        let mut off = 0;
        while off < src.len() {
            let dst = self.chunk_mut();
            let cnt = std::cmp::min(dst.len(), src.len() - off);

            unsafe {
                std::ptr::copy_nonoverlapping(
                    src.as_ptr().add(off),
                    dst.as_mut_ptr(),
                    cnt,
                );
                self.advance_mut(cnt);
            }
            off += cnt;
        }
    }

    /// Put a single byte.
    fn put_u8(&mut self, n: u8) {
        assert!(self.remaining_mut() >= 1, "buffer overflow");
        let dst = self.chunk_mut();
        unsafe {
            dst.write_byte(0, n);
            self.advance_mut(1);
        }
    }

    /// Put an i8.
    fn put_i8(&mut self, n: i8) {
        self.put_u8(n as u8);
    }

    /// Put a big-endian u16.
    fn put_u16(&mut self, n: u16) {
        self.put_slice(&n.to_be_bytes());
    }

    /// Put a little-endian u16.
    fn put_u16_le(&mut self, n: u16) {
        self.put_slice(&n.to_le_bytes());
    }

    /// Put a big-endian i16.
    fn put_i16(&mut self, n: i16) {
        self.put_u16(n as u16);
    }

    /// Put a little-endian i16.
    fn put_i16_le(&mut self, n: i16) {
        self.put_u16_le(n as u16);
    }

    /// Put a big-endian u32.
    fn put_u32(&mut self, n: u32) {
        self.put_slice(&n.to_be_bytes());
    }

    /// Put a little-endian u32.
    fn put_u32_le(&mut self, n: u32) {
        self.put_slice(&n.to_le_bytes());
    }

    /// Put a big-endian i32.
    fn put_i32(&mut self, n: i32) {
        self.put_u32(n as u32);
    }

    /// Put a little-endian i32.
    fn put_i32_le(&mut self, n: i32) {
        self.put_u32_le(n as u32);
    }

    /// Put a big-endian u64.
    fn put_u64(&mut self, n: u64) {
        self.put_slice(&n.to_be_bytes());
    }

    /// Put a little-endian u64.
    fn put_u64_le(&mut self, n: u64) {
        self.put_slice(&n.to_le_bytes());
    }

    /// Put a big-endian i64.
    fn put_i64(&mut self, n: i64) {
        self.put_u64(n as u64);
    }

    /// Put a little-endian i64.
    fn put_i64_le(&mut self, n: i64) {
        self.put_u64_le(n as u64);
    }

    /// Put a big-endian u128.
    fn put_u128(&mut self, n: u128) {
        self.put_slice(&n.to_be_bytes());
    }

    /// Put a little-endian u128.
    fn put_u128_le(&mut self, n: u128) {
        self.put_slice(&n.to_le_bytes());
    }

    /// Put a big-endian f32.
    fn put_f32(&mut self, n: f32) {
        self.put_u32(n.to_bits());
    }

    /// Put a little-endian f32.
    fn put_f32_le(&mut self, n: f32) {

    /// Put a big-endian f64.

    /// Limit writing to `limit` bytes.
```

```rust
// bytes/src/buf/impl_bytes.rs

impl Buf for Bytes {
    fn remaining(&self) -> usize {
        self.len()
    }

    fn chunk(&self) -> &[u8] {
        self.as_ref()
    }

    fn advance(&mut self, cnt: usize) {
        *self = self.slice(cnt..);
    }
}

impl Buf for &[u8] {
    fn remaining(&self) -> usize {
        self.len()
    }

    fn chunk(&self) -> &[u8] {
        self
    }

    fn advance(&mut self, cnt: usize) {
        *self = &self[cnt..];
    }
}

impl<const N: usize> Buf for &[u8; N] {
    fn remaining(&self) -> usize {
        N
    }

    fn chunk(&self) -> &[u8] {
        self.as_slice()
    }

    fn advance(&mut self, cnt: usize) {
        // Can't advance a fixed array reference, but needed for trait
        panic!("cannot advance array reference");
    }
}

impl BufMut for BytesMut {
    fn remaining_mut(&self) -> usize {
        // BytesMut can always grow
        usize::MAX - self.len()
    }

    fn chunk_mut(&mut self) -> &mut UninitSlice {
        if self.capacity() == self.len() {
            self.reserve(64); // Default growth
        }

        // Get uninit slice from capacity - len
        let cap = self.capacity();
        let len = self.len();
        let ptr = unsafe { self.as_mut_ptr().add(len) };

        unsafe {
            UninitSlice::from_raw_parts_mut(
                ptr as *mut MaybeUninit<u8>,
                cap - len,
            )
        }
    }

    unsafe fn advance_mut(&mut self, cnt: usize) {
        let len = self.len();
        self.set_len(len + cnt);
    }
}

impl BufMut for Vec<u8> {
    fn remaining_mut(&self) -> usize {
        usize::MAX - self.len()
    }

    fn chunk_mut(&mut self) -> &mut UninitSlice {
        if self.capacity() == self.len() {
            self.reserve(64);
        }

        let cap = self.capacity();
        let len = self.len();

        unsafe {
            UninitSlice::from_raw_parts_mut(
                self.as_mut_ptr().add(len) as *mut MaybeUninit<u8>,
                cap - len,
            )
        }
    }

    unsafe fn advance_mut(&mut self, cnt: usize) {
        let len = self.len();
        self.set_len(len + cnt);
    }
}

impl BufMut for &mut [u8] {
    fn remaining_mut(&self) -> usize {
        self.len()
    }

    fn chunk_mut(&mut self) -> &mut UninitSlice {
        unsafe {
            UninitSlice::from_raw_parts_mut(
                self.as_mut_ptr() as *mut MaybeUninit<u8>,
                self.len(),
            )
        }
    }

    unsafe fn advance_mut(&mut self, cnt: usize) {
        let ptr = self.as_mut_ptr();
        let len = self.len();
        *self = std::slice::from_raw_parts_mut(ptr.add(cnt), len - cnt);
    }
}
```
